segments.purgatorio-2-g112: Ed is a connective variant of e, here functioning as 'And' to begin the reported speech. elli is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun ('he'), and a is the preposition introducing the indirect object me ('to me'); the overall phrase 'Ed elli a me:' functions as 'And he to me:' = 'And he said to me:'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g113: This is an impersonal/passive-style statement: Nessun m’è fatto oltraggio literally 'No (one) to-me is made outrage'. m’è fatto is built with essere + past participle (fatto) to express 'has been done to me' (i.e. 'I have not been wronged').
segments.purgatorio-2-g114: This conditional/concessive clause begins with se ('if') and uses a relative 'quei che' = 'the one who'. leva is present indicative of levare ('lifts' / 'removes'); 'quando e cui li piace' is a terse idiomatic subordinate 'whenever and to whom it pleases (him)', where the indirect object marker (a) is omitted in poetry and li is enclitic/archaic. The whole clause qualifies the situation in which denial of passage would not be an outrage.
segments.purgatorio-2-g115: più volte is an adverbial phrase 'many times' or 'repeatedly'. m’ha negato is the compound perfect (ha + negato) with m' as indirect object 'to me', so 'has denied me' or 'has refused me'. esto is an archaic demonstrative 'this', and passaggio is the direct object 'passage' or 'permission to pass'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g116: ché = 'for/because'. di giusto voler is an idiomatic genitive/causal phrase 'out of/by [a] just will' (giusto modifies voler, 'just/right desire'). lo suo = 'his own' (lo + suo, a reinforced possessive), and si face is archaic present of fare with reflexive si ('he acts/makes himself' → 'he acts'), so the clause says he acts according to his just will.
segments.purgatorio-2-g117: veramente 'indeed' introduces actual fact. da tre mesi expresses a duration ('for three months'). elli ha tolto uses elli as subject and ha tolto (perfect of togliere) 'has taken away' or here 'has kept away / prevented'. chi ha voluto intrar is a free relative 'whoever has wished to enter', with intrar an archaic/informal infinitive form of entrare.
segments.purgatorio-2-g118: con tutta pace is an idiomatic closing adverbial 'with all peace' = 'in perfect calm' or 'peacefully / without disturbance'; con is the preposition, tutta intensifies pace ('completely'), and pace = 'peace/calm'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g119: Ond' is a poetic contraction of 'onde' used here as a discourse connective meaning 'therefore/so' at the start of the clause; 'io' is the emphatic subject pronoun. The comma after 'io' marks a pause and emphasizes the subject before the relative clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-2-g120: This is a relative clause introduced by 'ch’' (che = 'who/that') and predicated by the imperfect 'era' ('was'), which indicates the state or disposition of the subject at that time. 'Ora' means 'now' (at that moment); 'a la marina vòlto' is a phrase where 'a' marks direction/location ('toward/at'), 'la marina' = 'the seashore', and 'vòlto' is a past participle used adjectivally with 'era' to mean 'turned/facing'. 'Dove' introduces a subordinate locative clause: 'l'acqua di Tevero s'insala' — here the clitic 's'' is the reflexive/pronominal marker and 'insala' is a present-form verb meaning 'becomes salted/brackish' (the river's water is made brackish at that place); the elided article 'l' precedes 'acqua'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g121: The adverb 'benignamente' ('kindly/graciously') modifies the verbal phrase. 'Fu' is the contracted passato remoto third person singular of 'essere' ('was'), used here as the auxiliary in a passive construction; the contraction (apostrophe) is a metrical/orthographic shortening. 'Da' marks the agent in the passive ('by'), 'lui' is the agent pronoun ('him'), and 'ricolto' is the past participle ('gathered/taken up'), so the whole says 'was kindly gathered up by him.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g122: A quella foce is a prepositional phrase of place ('at/to that mouth'). The clause shows inverted word order: ha (present indicative of avere) is the verb, elli is an archaic third-person subject pronoun, or (adverb) means 'now', and dritta l'ala ('the wing straight') is the object phrase describing the wing's position. Together: 'he/it now has the wing straight toward that mouth.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g123: Però che introduces a causal clause ('for/because'), quivi is a locative adverb 'there', and si ricoglie is a reflexive present indicative meaning 'folds/gathers itself' or 'returns there'. Qual verso functions like 'to which direction/where' (introducing the subordinate idea of direction), verso + Acheronte means 'toward Acheron', and non si cala is a negated reflexive verb 'does not descend' — so the clause says that it always folds itself there, in the direction from which it does not descend toward Acheron.
segments.purgatorio-2-g124: E io is simply the coordinating conjunction 'and' plus the first-person pronoun 'I', marking the speaker's reply ('And I:').
segments.purgatorio-2-g125: This is a conditional subordinate clause introduced by Se ('if'). Nuova legge is 'new law/rule' (nuova modifies legge). Non ti toglie is present indicative of togliere with indirect object pronoun ti: 'does not take away from you'; memoria o uso are the direct objects 'memory or habit/practice'; a l'amoroso canto is a prepositional phrase specifying the domain ('of/regarding the amorous song'). The relative clause che mi solea quetar tutte mie doglie modifies canto: che (that) + mi (me, indirect object) + solea (imperfect of solere, 'used to') + quetar (infinitive 'to quiet', archaic spelling) + tutte mie doglie (direct object 'all my griefs').
segments.purgatorio-2-g126: Di ciò ('of/for that') refers back to the foregoing thought; ti piaccia is a polite/deliberative formula using piacere in the present subjunctive (third person singular) with ti as indirect object — literally 'may it be pleasing to you' i.e. 'please (would you)'; consolare is the infinitive 'to console/comfort'; alquanto 'somewhat' modifies the request; l'anima mia is the direct object 'my soul'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g127: Che here is a relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the clause that describes the state of the soul (it links to the following participial/gerund phrase).
segments.purgatorio-2-g128: Con la sua persona venendo qui is an accompanying/causal phrase: con + noun ('with his person') plus venendo, the gerund of venire ('coming'), used adverbially to mean 'with his person coming here' or 'by his coming here'. Qui is the locative adverb 'here'. The gerund links this circumstance to the main clause about the soul's distress.
segments.purgatorio-2-g129: È affannata tanto: è is the present indicative of essere ('is'); affannata is a past participle used predicatively/adjectivally meaning 'distressed/breathless/afflicted' and agrees with anima (feminine singular); tanto is an adverbial intensifier 'so much/so'. Together: 'is so distressed.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g130: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a full stop). It does not contain words to analyze; it simply marks a pause or end of the previous utterance.
segments.purgatorio-2-g131: The quoted phrase 'Amor che ne la mente mi ragiona' is a noun 'Amor' (personified) followed by a relative clause introduced by 'che' (who/that). 'Ne' here means 'in' (in the mind), 'la mente' is the noun 'the mind', and 'mi ragiona' is present indicative 'reasons/speaks to me' with 'mi' as indirect object. Outside the quote, 'cominciò' is the main verb in the passato remoto ('began'), with 'elli' as the subject pronoun (archaic form; here 'he') and the adverbial phrase 'allor sì dolcemente' ('then so sweetly') modifying 'cominciò' — the word order (verb before pronoun) and the archaic pronoun form are typical of medieval Italian.
segments.purgatorio-2-g132: The conjunction 'che' here introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'such that'). 'La dolcezza' is the grammatical subject ('the sweetness'), 'ancor' is an adverb meaning 'still', 'dentro' ('within') shows location, and 'mi suona' is present indicative 'rings/resounds in me' with 'mi' as the experiencer. The verb 'suona' is used metaphorically for an inner impression.
segments.purgatorio-2-g133: 'Lo mio maestro e io e quella gente' is a compound subject ('my master and I and that company'); the article 'Lo' before 'mio' is an older/poetic form. 'Ch’ eran con lui' is a relative clause ('who were with him') where 'ch’' is a contraction of 'che' and 'eran' is the imperfect 'were'. 'Parevan' is the imperfect 'appeared' agreeing in number with the compound subject, and 'sì contenti' describes the state ('so content').
segments.purgatorio-2-g134: The clause beginning with 'come' introduces a comparison ('as if'). 'A nessun' is an impersonal dative ('to no one'), and 'toccasse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'toccare' used here to express a hypothetical or potential action ('were touched' / 'might concern'). 'Altro' ('anything else') is the object and 'la mente' ('the mind') is the subject — the word order is inverted for emphasis, a typical poetic construction.
segments.purgatorio-2-g135: 'Noi eravam' is the imperfect 'we were'. 'Tutti fissi e attenti' describes the group as entirely 'fixed' (focused) and 'attentive'; 'a le sue note' (archaic separation of the article, i.e. 'alle sue note') means 'to his notes/sounds' — 'note' here refers to musical or vocal sounds. The clause is a simple descriptive past-state statement.
segments.purgatorio-2-g136: 'Ed ecco' is idiomatic ('and behold'); 'il veglio onesto' is an attributive noun phrase ('the honest old man') using an older spelling 'veglio' for 'vecchio'. 'Gridando' is a present participle ('crying out' / 'shouting') that describes how he speaks, functioning adverbially with the main verb implied ('he cried out').
segments.purgatorio-2-g137: This is the beginning of direct speech: 'Che è ciò,' literally 'What is this?' — 'Che' functions as an interrogative 'what', 'è' is present 'is', and 'ciò' is the demonstrative 'this/that'. The comma marks the pause before the vocative that follows.
segments.purgatorio-2-g138: The vocative 'spiriti lenti?' addresses the listeners as 'spirits' with the adjective 'lenti' ('slow' or 'sluggish'). 'Spiriti' here is the addressee noun; the provided lemma senses did not include the noun meaning 'spirits', so it is treated as unmatched.
segments.purgatorio-2-g22: Poi (then) is a temporal adverb that opens the clause. d’ogne is a contracted da + ogne (from every), introducing the prepositional phrase d’ogne lato (from every side) placed before the indirect object phrase ad esso (toward it). m’appario is the older/dialectal form of mi apparve (verb + clitic), a past tense (perfective) third-person form meaning “appeared to me.” The noun phrase un non sapeva che bianco follows the verb in Dante’s inverted/orderly style; here non sapeva che + adjective is an idiomatic, somewhat elliptical construction meaning “I/one could not say exactly what (it) was like” — so un (one) is qualified by the parenthetical non sapeva che bianco (“I couldn’t say what white it was”).
segments.purgatorio-2-g23: e (and) links the second clause. di sotto is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning “from below.” a poco a poco (literally “at little by little”) is a repetitive adverbial expression meaning “little by little” or “gradually.” The subject un altro (another one) precedes the verb uscìo, an archaic/past form (passato remoto) meaning “emerged” or “came forth,” and a lui indicates motion toward him (‘toward him’). The overall word order (subject before verb, verb final) is typical of Dante’s style.
segments.purgatorio-2-g24: Lo mio maestro (the + possessive + noun) is an older word order (Lo + mio + maestro) meaning “my master.” ancor = still; non facea motto uses the imperfect facea (fa-re) plus motto (“utterance/word”) to mean “was making no remark / made no sound” — the imperfect conveys a continuing state of silence.
segments.purgatorio-2-g25: mentre che introduces a temporal clause “while/whereas.” i primi bianchi (the first white ones) is the subject; apparver is an archaic past (passato remoto) third-person plural of apparire (they appeared/revealed), and ali (wings) follows as what was shown — i.e. “the first white ones displayed their wings.” The inversion and compressed expression are characteristic of Dante’s poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-2-g26: allor che (then/when) begins a new temporal clause. ben (well/clearly) modifies the verb conobbe, the passato remoto of conoscere used here to mean “recognized.” il galeotto (the ‘galeotto’) is the direct object; galeotto is a loaded, literary noun meaning a go‑between or pander (alluding to the Lancelot/Guinevere episode), and the phrase means “when he clearly recognized the pander.”
segments.purgatorio-2-g27: gridò is the passato remoto third-person singular of gridare, “he shouted/cried out.” Appearing alone with a colon, it introduces direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-2-g28: Fa is the imperative singular of fare (“do/make”), a direct command voiced by the speaker in the quotation; the repetition Fa, fa is emphatic, like “Do it, do it.”
segments.purgatorio-2-g29: The second fa is again an imperative used with che to introduce a subjunctive result clause: fa che + [subjunctive] = “make that …” le ginocchia (the knees) is the subject of the dependent clause, and cali is an older present-subjunctive form of calare (to lower) meaning “may lower / bend.” So fa che le ginocchia cali = “make his knees bend” (i.e. make him kneel).
segments.purgatorio-2-g30: Ecco is an interjection meaning 'behold' or 'here is' used to call attention; the article l' (il) is elided before the vowel of angel (poetic form for moderno 'angelo'); di marks possession or association ('of'), so Dio is the genitive 'of God'. The whole phrase is a short exclamatory NP introducing the figure.
segments.purgatorio-2-g31: Piega is the 2nd-person singular imperative (or present indicative 3rd sg form identical in form) of piegare; here it functions as a direct command addressed to the addressee: 'fold/bend'. Le is the definite article agreeing with mani (feminine plural), and mani is the plural noun 'hands'. The imperative + direct object gives the order to assume the prayer posture.
segments.purgatorio-2-g32: Omai is a temporal adverb ('now/by now/soon') that sets the time frame for the following future verb; vedrai is the future tense, 2nd-person singular of vedere, 'you will see'. Di introduces the phrase that follows in a partitive/relational sense ('of/about'); sì is an intensifier 'such/so', and fatti is the past participle fatto used adjectivally ('made/formed' → 'such-made' = 'such'), agreeing in sense with officiali (masculine plural). Officiali is a noun meaning 'officials' or ecclesiastical officers. The clause predicts that the listener will shortly see officials of that sort.
segments.purgatorio-2-g33: Vedi is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'see/notice' used to call attention. Che introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/which' and here introduces the content of what to notice. Sdegna is 3rd-person singular present indicative of sdegnare, 'he scorns/expresses disdain for'. Li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern Italian i/gli), agreeing with argomenti, which here means 'arguments/reasonings'; umani is the adjective 'human' agreeing in gender and number.
segments.purgatorio-2-g34: Sì che introduces a result clause and here means 'so that' or 'with the result that'. Remo is the noun 'oar' (no offered sense fits the listed lemma), so it is annotated as -1; non is the sentential negation 'not'. Vuol is 3rd-person singular present of volere and here means 'wants' or, with the negation, 'does not want/does not accept' — i.e. he refuses to use an oar. The clause therefore reports the practical consequence of his disdain.
segments.purgatorio-2-g35: Né is the coordinating negative 'nor' (introducing another negative alternative). Altro means 'other/any other'. Velo here is a metaphorical 'sail/covering' (literal is 'veil/covering'); che functions as a comparative 'than' in this context. L' is the elided article before ali (wings), ali is plural 'wings', and sue is the feminine/masculine plural possessive 'his/her/its' agreeing with ali. The phrase denies any sail or covering other than his wings.
segments.purgatorio-2-g36: Tra is the preposition 'among/between'. Liti is the plural of lite, 'quarrels/disputes' (here meaning conflicts or contests). Sì is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'so very', and lontani is the adjective 'distant/remote' agreeing in number and gender. The prepositional phrase places the action amid/among disputes that are far apart (or distant in nature).
segments.purgatorio-2-g100: Rispuosemi is rispose (passato remoto 'answered') with the enclitic object pronoun -mi attached; the verb appears before any explicit subject (typical in narrative) and yields 'answered me.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g101: Così com' is a contracted comparative 'così come' = 'just as'; io is the subject pronoun 'I'; t' is the enclitic object pronoun 'te' (here 'you') attached to the verb amare; amai is passato remoto (I loved); 'nel mortal corpo' is a prepositional phrase ('in the mortal/body') modifying the verb.
segments.purgatorio-2-g102: Così begins the parallel clause 'so/thus'; t' is again the enclitic object pronoun 'you'; amo is present indicative 'I love' (showing continuing love); sciolta is a past participle/adjective ('loosed, freed') placed after the verb and agreeing in sense with the person referred to, giving 'I love you freed.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g103: Però is a discourse particle here meaning 'therefore/so'; m'arresto is mi arresto with the reflexive pronoun enclitic (m') and the verb arresto (I stop / I halt), so the clause means 'therefore I stay/stop.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g104: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'; tu is the emphatic subject pronoun 'you'; perché is the interrogative 'why'; vai is present indicative 2nd person singular 'you go/are going' forming the question 'but you, why are you going?'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g105: Punctuation only: a full stop marking the end of the line/sentence.
segments.purgatorio-2-g106: Casella functions here as a vocative (a proper name addressed directly); mio follows as an affectionate possessive/term of address ('my Casella'). The lemma given ('casella' as 'small box') does not reflect the proper-name use, so the sense is not one of the listed nominal senses.
segments.purgatorio-2-g107: Per + infinitive (per tornar) expresses purpose or motive 'in order to return'; altra volta = 'another time/once more'; là dov' io son is a relative clause 'there where I am' (dov' = dove, 'where') with son = present 'I am.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g108: Fo is an archaic first-person present of fare used like 'I make/do' ('I make this journey'); io is an emphatic subject pronoun placed after the verb; questo viaggio is the direct object 'this journey' (vïaggio uses a dieresis marking pronunciation).
segments.purgatorio-2-g109: Diss' io is a contracted past-tense reporting formula (disse io) meaning 'I said' with the subject pronoun repeated after the verb for emphasis; diss' is the archaic/poetic past form.
segments.purgatorio-2-g110: Ma introduces the question 'but'; a te ('to you' / 'for you') indicates the person affected; com' (come) is 'how' and è is present 'is/has been' in a passive/perfect sense; 'tanta ora tolta' = 'such an hour taken away' with tolta a past participle agreeing with ora (feminine), so the clause asks 'but how has such an hour been taken from you?'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g111: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period/full stop) that closes the preceding line; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-2-g84: A direct address/cry. "Ohi" is an interjection (vocative exclamation) placed before the noun. "ombre vane" is a noun phrase: "ombre" (shades, i.e. souls) with the adjective "vane" (empty/vain) after it; the speaker omits an explicit verb and simply calls to them.
segments.purgatorio-2-g85: "Fuor che" here works as an exception clause (literally 'except that' / 'save that'). "ne l’aspetto" is a prepositional phrase: "ne" = 'in' (here marking the domain), "l’" = the definite article "il", and "aspetto" = 'appearance'. The whole segment qualifies the preceding address: they are vain only in appearance.
segments.purgatorio-2-g86: "Tre volte" is a numeral phrase meaning 'three times' and functions adverbially. Word order places the adverbial phrases before the verb; the subject "io" is omitted (typical in Italian). "Dietro a lei" ('behind her') indicates position relative to her, "le mani" is the direct object, and "avvinsi" is passato remoto, first-person singular — 'I clasped/entwined (my hands around hers)'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g87: The conjunction "e" links the second clause. "Tante" refers back to the number just stated ('as many [times]'). "Mi" is a clitic marking the first-person affected by the action, and "tornai" is passato remoto first-person singular 'I returned/brought back'. "Con esse" uses the pronoun for the hands ('with them'), and "al petto" ('to the chest') is the goal. The clause describes a repeated reciprocal action.
segments.purgatorio-2-g88: "Di maraviglia" is an ablative/causal phrase ('out of/from wonder' or 'with amazement') functioning adverbially to indicate cause or manner for what follows.
segments.purgatorio-2-g89: "Credo" is the present indicative first-person singular of "credere" and here parenthetically means 'I believe' or 'I think', marking the speaker's uncertainty about his own impression.
segments.purgatorio-2-g90: "Mi dipinsi" is passato remoto first-person singular with a clitic "mi"; literally 'I painted myself', used figuratively ('I assumed/expressed [an air] of amazement' or 'I depicted myself in wonder'). The reflexive clitic makes the verb middle/figurative.
segments.purgatorio-2-g91: "Per che" is an older/poetic connective meaning 'whereupon' / 'so/therefore'. "L’ombra" is the subject article+noun 'the shade', "sorrise" is passato remoto third-person singular 'smiled', and "si ritrasse" is the reflexive passato remoto 'withdrew/drew back' (the reflexive "si" marks the verb as intransitive/reflexive).
segments.purgatorio-2-g92: "E io," is simply the conjunction 'and' plus the emphatic subject pronoun 'I' introduced to mark the change of perspective or action by the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-2-g93: "Seguendo lei" is a present participial phrase ('following her') that functions adverbially to indicate the manner in which the narrator acts; the participle precedes the main verb it modifies.
segments.purgatorio-2-g94: "Oltre" ('beyond/further') is an adverb marking the direction/extent of motion. "Mi" is the clitic for the first person, and "pinsi" is passato remoto first-person singular of a poetic verb meaning 'I urged/pressed myself onward' (here: 'I pushed myself further'). The verb combination expresses the narrator's forward movement following her.
segments.purgatorio-2-g95: "Soavemente" is an adverb ('sweetly/gently') modifying the verb "disse" (passato remoto, 'said'). The subordinate clause "ch’io posasse" uses "ch’" (che) introducing a subjunctive: "posasse" is the imperfect subjunctive first-person singular of "posare" and follows the past-tense main verb, giving reported advice/command meaning 'that I should pause/rest.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g96: "Allor" (archaic for 'then') introduces the next action. "Conobbi" is passato remoto first-person singular of "conoscere" meaning 'I recognized' (or 'I came to know'), and "chi era" is an indirect question clause 'who she was' functioning as its object/complement.
segments.purgatorio-2-g97: The conjunction "e" links clauses. "Pregai" is passato remoto first-person singular of "pregare" meaning 'I begged/asked' (here 'I begged/pleaded'). "Che" introduces a substantive subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive.
segments.purgatorio-2-g98: "Per" introduces purpose ('in order to'), and "parlarmi" is the infinitive "parlare" with the enclitic "-mi" attached ('to speak to me'). The phrase marks the reason/purpose for the requested action in the subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-2-g99: "Un poco" ('a little') is an adverbial quantifier modifying the verb. "S’" is the reflexive clitic and "arrestasse" is the imperfect subjunctive third-person singular of "arrestare" used in the subordinate clause after "pregai che"; it expresses the request that she 'stop' or 'remain' for a short time.
segments.purgatorio-2-g37: Vedi is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'see' or 'behold'. come introduces an exclamatory/comparative clause 'how' (how he does it). l’ is the enclitic object pronoun (here = 'le' referring back to the wings) attached to the verb ha ('has'/'holds'); dritte is an adjective (feminine plural) agreeing with that object and describes their position ('straight/upright'); verso ’l cielo is a prepositional phrase 'toward the sky/heaven' modifying the whole verb phrase.
segments.purgatorio-2-g38: trattando is a present gerund ('treating/handling') that links to the main clause and describes the manner of action. l’aere is the direct object of the gerund ('the air'). con introduces the instrument ('with'), and l’etterne penne ('his/ the eternal feathers') names the means used; etterne is an archaic adjective meaning 'eternal/everlasting' and penne here means 'feathers' (the bird's plumage).
segments.purgatorio-2-g39: che begins a relative clause ('which') referring back to penne. non si mutan is a present-tense, 3rd-person plural verb with the reflexive clitic si ('they do not change themselves' = 'they do not change/molt'); come introduces the comparison 'like' and mortal pelo is the standard of comparison ('mortal hair' = human hair). The verb mutan is an archaic present form (modern mutano).
segments.purgatorio-2-g40: Poi is a simple temporal adverb 'then/afterwards' that opens this sequence of events.
segments.purgatorio-2-g41: come ... venne is a circumstantial clause introduced by come ('as'/'when') describing simultaneous action: 'as the divine bird came more and more toward us'. più e più is an emphatic repetition meaning 'more and more'; verso noi is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward us'; venne is the past-tense (preterite/simple past in Dante) of venire 'came'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g42: più chiaro appariva is an imperfect (appariva) describing an ongoing state: 'it appeared the clearer' (i.e. became clearer to the eye as it approached). più modifies chiaro 'clear/brighter', and appariva is 'appeared/seemed'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g43: per che introduces a consequence ('so/therefore'), followed by l’occhio 'the eye' as subject. da presso means 'close up/at close range'; nol is the contracted negative clitic (non + lo = 'not it' → 'could not it'), and sostenne is the perfect/past of sostenere used here with the sense 'to endure/support' ('the eye could not endure it up close').
segments.purgatorio-2-g44: ma = 'but'; chinail is an archaic/poetic contracted verbal form meaning 'bowed it' or 'bent it' (chinò + lo), so chinail giuso means 'bowed it down' or 'lowered it down' — giuso = 'down'. This describes the eye being lowered.
segments.purgatorio-2-g45: e quei sen venne a riva: e = 'and'; quei (here) = 'that one' or 'it/that' referring to the bird; sen is the contracted se n' (se + ne) meaning 'away' or 'off' (so 'se n' venne' = 'went off/away'); venne is 'came' (past); a riva = 'to the shore'. con un vasello snelletto e leggero is an instrumental phrase 'with a small, nimble and light boat' (un vasello = small boat; snelletto is an adjective meaning quick/slender/nimble; leggero = light).
segments.purgatorio-2-g46: tanto che introduces a result clause 'so that' (tanto che ...), and l’acqua nulla ne ’nghiottiva is the result: 'the water swallowed none of it.' nulla = 'nothing/none'; ne is a clitic referring back to the boat or its weight/volume ('of it'); ’nghiottiva is an archaic/contraction of inghiottiva 'swallowed' (imperfect), so the water did not engulf/swallow any of it.
segments.purgatorio-2-g47: Da poppa is a locative phrase 'from the stern' (poppa = stern of the ship). stava is an imperfect of stare meaning 'stood' or 'was standing/was situated'; il celestial nocchiero is the subject 'the heavenly helmsman' (nocchiero = helmsman/pilot).
segments.purgatorio-2-g48: tal che introduces a result/characterizing clause 'such that...'. faria is an archaic conditional (modern farebbe) 'would make', beato here is used as a verb sense in the provided lexicon ('make blessed' / 'beatify' or 'would make one blessed'), pur = 'even/still', descripto = past participle 'described'. The construction implies 'so (splendid) that he would make blessed even when merely described' (i.e. his beauty/majesty is such that description alone would render one blessed).
segments.purgatorio-2-g49: e = 'and'; più di cento = 'more than a hundred'; spirti is the plural 'spirits' (souls); entro sediero is a phrase with entro 'within' and sediero an archaic past form meaning 'they sat' (sedettero) — together: 'more than a hundred spirits sat within.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g50: The quoted Latin line 'In exitu Isräel de Aegypto' is the opening of the psalm being cited. cantavan is an imperfect plural 'they were singing' or 'they sang', tutti insieme ad una voce = 'all together with one voice' (tutti = all, insieme = together, ad una voce = in unison). con quanto di quel salmo è poscia scripto literally means 'with all that of that psalm is later written' — quanto = 'all that' (i.e. everything contained in the psalm), di quel salmo = 'of that psalm', è poscia scripto = 'is afterwards written' (poscia = afterwards, scripto = written).
segments.purgatorio-2-g68: Dianzi is an adverb meaning "earlier/before," placed before the verb for emphasis. Venimmo is the passato remoto (first-person plural) of venire, "we came/arrived."
segments.purgatorio-2-g69: Innanzi a voi means "before/in front of you" with innanzi (preposition/adverb) + a + voi; un poco follows to qualify how far/when ("a little/just a bit"). Word order places the adverbial phrase after the prepositional phrase.
segments.purgatorio-2-g70: Per altra via uses the preposition per ("by/through") to indicate means; altra via = "another way/route," with altra agreeing with via.
segments.purgatorio-2-g71: Che begins a relative clause "which/that." Fu is the passato remoto (third singular) of essere, "was." Sì ... che is the correlative construction "so ... that;" aspra e forte are coordinate adjectives describing the route as "harsh/rough and severe/strong."
segments.purgatorio-2-g72: Che introduces the result clause "that." Lo salire is a nominalized infinitive meaning "the ascent/the climbing" (lo = definite article + salire = to climb). Omai = "now/at this point." Ne is a clitic referring back to the matter at hand (roughly "of it/to that"), and parrà is the future of parere (here "will seem"); gioco = "a game." The whole means "that the ascent will now seem like a game."
segments.purgatorio-2-g73: L’ is the definite article elided before a vowel; anime is the plural of anima, "souls/spirits." The phrase marks the subject of the clause.
segments.purgatorio-2-g74: Che introduces a relative clause "who/that." Si is the reflexive/clitic pronoun used with accorte (they made themselves aware). Fuor is an archaic/poetic past form here functioning with the participle to indicate a past state (roughly corresponding to "they were/they became" in modern Italian). Di me = "of me/about me," and accorte (feminine plural) means "aware/noticing," agreeing with anime.
segments.purgatorio-2-g75: Per lo spirare means "by/through the breathing" or "on account of my breathing." Per marks cause/means, lo is the article, and spirare is a noun/gerund-like infinitive here meaning "breathing/expiration."
segments.purgatorio-2-g76: Ch’ (che) introduces the explanatory subordinate clause "that"; i’ is the archaic first-person pronoun "I;" era is the imperfect (here used as the simple past form in Dante) of essere, "was;" ancor = "still;" vivo = "alive." So: "that I was still alive."
segments.purgatorio-2-g77: Maravigliando is a present participle/adverbial gerund meaning "marveling/astonished." Diventaro is the passato remoto (third-person plural) of diventare, "they became." Smorte (feminine plural) means "pale/dim/insensible," agreeing with the feminine plural anime. The sequence shows concomitant action: amazed, they grew faint.
segments.purgatorio-2-g78: E come = "and as/just as." A messagger = "to a messenger" or "when a messenger" (a introduces the agent in this simile). Che porta ulivo is a relative clause "who carries/ brings an olive (branch)" (porta = present tense "carries"). Tragge la gente = archaic present "draws/ leads the people" (tragge = leads), per udir novelle = "to hear news," with per + infinitive udir indicating purpose and novelle (plural) meaning "new things/news."
segments.purgatorio-2-g79: E di calcar nessun si mostra schivo: e = "and." Di calcar here introduces the notion of "to press/tread on" (di + infinitive functions like an object or complement). Nessun si mostra schivo = "no one shows himself averse/reluctant," with si reflexive and mostrare used intransitively to mean "appear/show oneself."
segments.purgatorio-2-g80: Così al viso mio s’affisar quelle anime fortunate tutte quante: così = "thus/so." Al viso mio = "on/at my face" (meaning "at my face/at my countenance/gaze"). S’affisar is an archaic/clitic past form meaning "fixed themselves upon" (s’ = reflexive clitic attached to the verb), quelle anime fortunate = "those blessed/fortunate souls," tutte quante = "every one of them/all of them."
segments.purgatorio-2-g81: Quasi oblïando = "almost/as if forgetting," with oblïando a present participle meaning "forgetting/neglecting." D’ire a farsi belle = "to go to make themselves fair/beautiful," where d’ (di) + ire (to go) + a + farsi (reflexive "to make oneself") + belle (feminine plural) describes the purpose they almost neglect.
segments.purgatorio-2-g82: Io vidi = "I saw" (vidi = passato remoto of vedere). Una di lor = "one of them" (lor = clitic/poetic form of loro). Trarresi avante is reflexive: trarresi (archaic past/reflexive) + avante "forward," meaning she drew herself forward/stepped forward. Per abbracciarmi = "in order to embrace me" (per + infinitive expresses purpose), con sì grande affetto = "with such great affection."
segments.purgatorio-2-g83: Che introduces a result clause "that." Mosse is the passato remoto (third singular) of muovere, "(she) moved/prompted." Me = "me" (direct/indirect object). A far lo somigliante = "to make/do the like/the same" (a + infinitive far = "to do/make," lo somigliante = "the similar/like thing"), so the clause means "that moved me to do likewise."
segments.purgatorio-2-g51: Poi is a temporal adverb meaning 'then'. Fece is passato remoto (3rd singular) of fare, 'made/did.' Il segno lor di santa croce is the direct object; lor is the enclitic poetic form of loro and functions as an indirect object ('to them' / 'over them'). The whole phrase = 'then he made the sign of the holy cross (over them)'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g52: Ond' is a contracted/dialectal form (onde/ond) meaning 'whereupon/therefore' and introduces the consequence. Ei is a literary form for 'they.' Si is the reflexive pronoun; gittar is an archaic/poetic past form of gettare (here used as a past action, 3rd plural), 'threw/threw themselves.' Tutti in su la piaggia indicates direction ('all up onto the slope').
segments.purgatorio-2-g53: Ed is the conjunction 'and.' El sen gí is elliptical/poetic for 'egli se ne andò' (se n' andò) — sen is the fused form of se + ne and gí is a past form meaning 'went' or 'went away.' The phrase means 'and he went away.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g54: Come introduces a manner comparison ('as/just as'). Venne is passato remoto (3rd singular) of venire, 'he came.' Together come venne = 'as he came' (i.e., in the same way/manner as he had come).
segments.purgatorio-2-g55: Veloce is an adjective used adverbially here, modifying the manner of his going ('swift' / 'quickly'). It completes the comparative construction begun by come venne.
segments.purgatorio-2-g56: La turba is the noun phrase 'the crowd.' Che is a relative pronoun introducing the relative clause; rimase is passato remoto (3rd singular) of rimanere, 'remained'; lì is the locative adverb 'there.' The whole unit = 'the crowd that remained there.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g57: Selvaggia is an adjective 'wild/savage' placed before the verb for emphasis. Parea is the imperfect/poetic form of parere (3rd singular), 'seemed/appeared.' Del loco ('of the place') gives the reason or quality tied to the location: 'seemed savage from that place.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g58: Rimirando is a gerund ('gazing/looking intently'), and intorno is its adverbial complement ('around'). Come introduces a simile: colui (that one/he who) + che (relative) + nove cose assaggia is a comparative clause; assaggia is present indicative of assaggiare ('tastes/samples'), used here to paint a vivid likeness ('like one who samples nine things').
segments.purgatorio-2-g59: Da tutte parti is a circumstantial phrase 'from all parts/from every direction.' Saettava is an imperfect/poetic form of saettare (3rd singular) meaning 'shot/was shooting (arrows or rays).' Il giorno lo sol is an inverted word order; lo sol = 'the sun' (poetic), and giorno relates to the time/frame 'through the day.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g60: Ch' is the relative 'which/that.' Avea is the imperfect auxiliary 'had' (3rd singular). Con le saette conte is an instrumental phrase 'with the counted/measured shafts' (conte is an archaic/poetic form related to 'contate'/'counted'), and cacciato is a past participle 'driven/expelled.' Capricorno is the direct object: 'which with its measured arrows had driven Capricorn from the middle of the sky.' Grammatically this entire clause modifies the preceding sun/day clause.
segments.purgatorio-2-g61: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when.' La nova gente = 'the new people' (those who had just appeared). Alzò is passato remoto (3rd singular) of alzare, 'raised/lifted.' La fronte 'the face/forehead' and ver' (verso contracted) = 'toward' us (noi). Ver' is an archaic contraction of verso and thus directional; it is not the modern word 'ver' meaning 'true.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g62: Dicendo is a gerund ('saying'), and a noi marks the indirect object 'to us.' The gerund frames the reported speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-2-g63: Se is the conditional/if particle 'if' (here introducing an interrogative conditional). Voi is the 2nd-person plural pronoun 'you.' Sapete is present indicative (2nd pl) of sapere, 'know.' Together 'Se voi sapete' = 'If you know.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g64: Mostratene is an imperative form (mostrate → mostratene) with the enclitic pronoun -ne meaning 'of it/of them' or 'to us of it' depending on sense; here it functions as 'show us.' La via di gire al monte is the noun phrase 'the way to go to the mountain' where gire is the infinitive 'to go' and di marks the purpose/route. The whole is the request 'show us the way to go to the mountain.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g65: E is the conjunction 'and.' Virgilio is the subject proper name. Rispuose is passato remoto (3rd singular) of rispondere (here in the spelling rispuose), 'answered/replied.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g66: Voi credete forse che is a rhetorical question opener: Voi credete (present 2nd pl) 'do you believe,' forse 'perhaps.' Che introduces the subordinate clause. Siamo is present indicative (1st pl) of essere 'we are.' Esperti d'esto loco = 'experienced in/with this place'; esto is an archaic form of questo ('this').
segments.purgatorio-2-g67: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but.' Noi is the subject pronoun 'we.' Siam is a contracted/poetic form of siamo (present indicative 'are'). Peregrin is a predicate noun 'pilgrims' (Dante uses the older form). Come voi siete is a comparison clause 'as you are' with siete present (2nd pl) of essere.
segments.purgatorio-2-g1: The clause begins with the temporal adverb 'Già' (already). 'era ... giunto' uses the imperfect auxiliary 'era' + past participle 'giunto' to form a past perfect/remote sense ('had reached' / 'was come'), common in older Italian. 'lo cui meridïan cerchio coverchia Ierusalèm col suo più alto punto' is a relative clause introduced by 'cui' (whose) modifying 'orizzonte'; word order is inverted for poetic effect (the relative clause follows immediately after the head noun).
segments.purgatorio-2-g2: 'e la notte' is a simple coordinate nominal phrase ('and the night'); 'e' links this clause to the previous one. The article 'la' marks 'notte' as the subject for the following relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-2-g3: This is a relative clause ('che opposita a lui cerchia') modifying 'la notte'. 'opposita' is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'opposite to'; 'a lui' = 'to it' (the sun), and 'cerchia' is present indicative of the verb 'cerchiare' meaning 'encircles/girds'. Word order places the adjective before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-2-g4: 'uscia ... fuor' is an archaic/poetic past form (uscìa) meaning 'came/got out'; 'di Gange' specifies origin ('from the Ganges'); 'con le Bilance' means 'with the Scales' (the constellation Libra). The sequence places source and accompanying sign after the verb for metrical reasons.
segments.purgatorio-2-g5: This relative clause ('che le caggion di man quando soverchia') further describes the 'Bilance'. 'quando soverchia' means 'when it overbalances/overweighs'; 'caggion di man' is an older phrasing indicating what happens to the hands (they 'fall' or are 'let go') when the scales are overburdened. The verb/nominal form 'caggion' is archaic.
segments.purgatorio-2-g6: 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'); the clause states the consequence for 'le bianche e le vermiglie guance' (the white and ruddy cheeks). Adjectives precede the noun for poetic rhythm ('le bianche e le vermiglie guance' = 'the white and the red/ruddy cheeks').
segments.purgatorio-2-g7: 'là dov’ i’ era' is a locative/subordinate clause 'there where I was'. 'dov’' is a contraction of 'dove' (where). The token listed as 'i' in the word list corresponds to the first-person pronoun 'I' (io) in the text (an archaic contraction i'), so the clause means 'where I was'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g8: The phrase 'de la bella Aurora per troppa etate divenivan rance' gives the reason/result: 'from the fair Dawn, through excessive age, they became rance.' 'per' here means 'because of' or 'through', 'divenivan' is imperfect 'were becoming', and 'rance' (archaic) means 'rancid/sallow/dulled'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g9: 'Noi eravam' is the imperfect indicative 'we were'. 'lunghesso' is an archaic compound/adverb meaning 'far along it' or 'a long way out' and modifies 'mare' (sea); 'ancora' means 'still'. The overall structure states location + continuing condition.
segments.purgatorio-2-g10: 'come' introduces a simile ('like people'). 'gente che pensa a suo cammino' is a relative clause: 'people who think about their own journey'; 'pensa' is present indicative (habitual/thought), and 'a suo cammino' uses 'a' + noun to mark the object of thought.
segments.purgatorio-2-g11: The second relative clause 'che va col cuore e col corpo dimora' explains the people: 'who go with the heart and whose body remains.' 'va' (goes) is present indicative; 'col cuore' is figurative ('with the heart' = their attention goes), while 'col corpo dimora' uses 'dimora' (stays/remains) to state that the body lingers.
segments.purgatorio-2-g12: 'Ed ecco' is a presentational phrase ('and behold' / 'and lo') used to call attention to what follows; 'Ed' is a variant of 'e' and 'ecco' is the demonstrative/exclamatory 'here is'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g13: 'qual' here begins a comparative clause ('such as' / 'just as'); it will be followed by the exemplar being compared (Mars at morning). It's an archaic/poetic form equivalent to 'come' or 'come quel'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g14: 'sorpreso dal mattino' is a participial phrase meaning 'overtaken/surprised by the morning'; 'sorpreso' is a past participle used adjectivally and 'dal' = 'da + il' = 'by the'. It qualifies the following image of Mars.
segments.purgatorio-2-g15: This descriptive clause ('per li grossi vapor Marte rosseggia giù nel ponente sovra ’l suol marino') states the image: 'through the heavy vapors Mars reddens down in the west above the sea-floor.' 'per' = 'through/by means of', 'rosseggia' is present indicative ('glows/reddens'), and the locatives ('giù nel ponente', 'sovra ’l suol marino') place the phenomenon in the western sky over the sea.
segments.purgatorio-2-g16: 'cotal m’apparve' ('so/thus it appeared to me') uses the demonstrative/adverb 'cotal' ('in such a way') + the clitic 'm'' = 'mi' ('to me') and 'apparve' (perfect of 'apparire' = 'appeared'). The subject is the previously evoked image; the verb is simple past.
segments.purgatorio-2-g17: The parenthetical 's’io ancor lo veggia' is a conditional '(if) I still see it' introduced by the contraction 's’' = 'se' (if). 'ancor' = 'still', 'lo' = direct object 'it', and 'veggia' is present subjunctive or rarer present form used here in an optative/conditional sense ('if I still perceive it').
segments.purgatorio-2-g18: 'un lume per lo mar venir sì ratto' is a nominal clause describing the apparition: 'a light coming over the sea so swiftly.' 'venir' is the infinitive used after the noun to indicate motion 'to come', and 'sì ratto' is an adverbial phrase 'so quick(ly)'; adjective order 'sì ratto' follows the noun for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-2-g19: 'che ’l muover suo nessun volar pareggia' is a result/comparison clause introduced by 'che' = 'that/so that/so...that'. '’l muover suo' = 'its motion', and 'nessun volar pareggia' means 'no flying (i.e., no flight) equals it' — 'pareggia' is present indicative 'equals/balances'. The sentence uses poetic noun forms like 'volar' (infinitive used substantively).
segments.purgatorio-2-g20:
'Dal qual' = 'from which/after which' introduces the clause referring to the previous apparition. 'com’ io un poco ebbi ritratto l’occhio' uses 'com
't = 'as' and the perfect 'ebbi ritratto' (I had withdrawn) — 'ebbi' + past participle is a literary perfect; 'ritratto l’occhio' = 'I had drawn back my eye'. 'per domandar lo duca mio' is a purpose phrase: 'in order to call/ask for my leader (guide)'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g21: 'rividil più lucente e maggior fatto' is compact: 'rividil' = 'I saw him again' (riveder / rivedere), the clitic -il = 'him'; 'più lucente' = 'more gleaming/brighter'; 'e maggior fatto' = 'and greater-made' i.e. 'grown larger' (here 'fatto' used as 'become/made' — 'grown'). Adjectives follow the verb for emphasis; 'fatto' is used in the sense 'grown'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g139: Qual here is an exclamatory interrogative adjective meaning 'what (kind of)', placed before the noun; negligenza is a feminine singular noun meaning 'negligence' or 'neglect' used in an exclamatory phrase.
segments.purgatorio-2-g140: Quale is again the interrogative adjective 'what kind of'; stare is used as a noun-like notion 'state/condition/behaviour' derived from the verb 'stare' (Dantean usage), è is the present indicative of essere 'is', and questo is the demonstrative pronoun 'this' — together forming 'what kind of state is this?'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g141: Correte is the plural imperative (or present addressing 'you' plural) meaning 'run!' or 'hurry!'; al monte is the prepositional phrase 'to the mountain'; a + infinitive (a spogliarvi) marks purpose 'to/so as to strip', with spogliarvi the infinitive 'to strip' carrying the clitic vi (reflexive/benefactive 'yourselves' / 'from you'); lo scoglio is the direct object 'the rock'; ch' is the contracted relative pronoun che 'which/that'; esser is the infinitive 'to be' (here the infinitive in the subordinate clause); non lascia is 'does not allow / does not let' (lasciare + infinitive); a voi places the indirect object 'to you'; Dio manifesto has an inverted order where manifesto (adjective 'manifest/evident') qualifies Dio: 'God manifest to you'. The relative clause literally renders 'which does not let God be manifest to you.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g142: Come quando is the simile introducer 'just as when' (come = 'as', quando = 'when'), beginning the comparative scene.
segments.purgatorio-2-g143: Cogliendo is the present participle of cogliere 'gathering' (setting the ongoing action); biado 'biado' is the crop/fodder grain here, and loglio is 'darnel' (a weed) joined by the coordinating o 'or'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g144: Li is the masculine plural article 'the'; colombi is the noun 'doves' (here the provided sense list does not include the avian meaning, so index is -1); adunati is a past participle used adjectivally 'gathered'; a la pastura is the prepositional phrase 'at/to the pasture'.
segments.purgatorio-2-g145: Queti (modern quieti/quieti) is an adjective meaning 'quiet' or 'calm' describing the doves; the provided senses list does not include this meaning, so the sense index is -1.
segments.purgatorio-2-g146: Sanza is an archaic form of senza 'without'; mostrar is the infinitive 'to show'; l' (the) + usato 'customary' form an adjectival phrase 'their usual/customary'; orgoglio is the noun 'pride' — together 'without showing their usual pride.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g147: Se is the conditional conjunction 'if'; cosa 'something' is the subject of appare 'appears' (present indicative); ond' is a contracted form of onde 'from which/whereby'; elli is the archaic third-person plural pronoun 'they'; abbian is the present subjunctive of avere ('they may have' / 'they feel') used here with paura 'fear' — together 'if something appears that makes them afraid.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g148: Subitamente is the adverb 'suddenly'; lasciano is present indicative 'they leave/abandon'; star is the infinitive 'to remain/be' used after lasciare in the sense 'leave X be' (here 'abandon the bait'); l' esca 'the bait' is the direct object.
segments.purgatorio-2-g149: Perch' is the contracted perché 'because'; assaliti is a past participle used in a passive/periphrastic construction 'are assailed/attacked'; son is the contracted son for sono 'are'; da indicates agent or cause 'by/from'; maggior is the comparative 'greater'; cura is 'care/anxiety' — 'because they are assailed by greater concern.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g150: Così 'thus/so'; vid' is the archaic short form of vidi 'I saw' (past tense); io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'; quella masnada fresca 'that fresh band/pack' is the direct object; lasciar lo canto uses the infinitive 'to abandon' with lo canto 'the song' as object.
segments.purgatorio-2-g151: E 'and' coordinates the next verb; fuggir is an infinitive 'to flee' used here in narrative sequence parallel to lasciar; ver' is a poetic contraction of verso 'toward' (the provided sense list does not capture this directional meaning, so index is -1); la costa 'the coast/slope' is the goal 'toward the shore/slope.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g152: Com' is the contracted come 'as/like'; om is the archaic form of uomo 'man' or 'one'; che is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; va is the present indicative 'goes' — 'like a man who goes.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g153: Né is the negative conjunction 'nor'; sa is the present of sapere 'knows'; dove 'where'; rïesca is an archaic subjunctive form meaning roughly 'may return / may get back' (the provided sense list does not match this meaning, so index -1) — 'nor knows where he may return/come back.'
segments.purgatorio-2-g154: Né again 'nor'; la nostra partita 'our departure' (partita here = departure); fu is the perfect/past of essere 'was'; men 'less'; tosta 'tosta' = 'quick/swift' (here 'not less swift') — the whole means 'nor was our departure any less swift.'

sentences.purgatorio-2-s29: And he said to me: "I have suffered no outrage, if the one who lifts (or releases) [them] whenever and to whomever he pleases has repeatedly denied me this passage; for he acts from his own just will: truly, for three months he has kept out whoever wished to enter, with complete calm."
sentences.purgatorio-2-s30: So I, who was now turned toward the seashore where the water of the Tiber grows brackish, was kindly gathered up by him.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s31: To that mouth he now has his wing straight, for it always folds itself there, whence it does not descend toward Acheron.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s32: And I: 'If no new rule has taken from you the memory or practice of the amorous song that used to quiet all my griefs, please, for that reason, console my soul a little; which, with his person coming here, is so distressed!'
sentences.purgatorio-2-s34: 'Love that reasons in my mind,' he then began so sweetly that the sweetness still rings within me.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s35: My master and I and that company who were with him appeared so content, as if no one's mind were touched by anything else.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s36: We were all fixed and attentive to his notes; and behold the honest old man, crying out: 'What is this, sluggish spirits?'
sentences.purgatorio-2-s5: Then, from every side toward it there appeared to me one — I could not tell what — white, and from below, little by little, another came forth toward him.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s6: My master still made no sound, while the first white ones showed their wings; when he well recognized the pander, he cried out: "Do — do, make him bend his knees."
sentences.purgatorio-2-s7: Behold the angel of God: fold your hands; you will soon see officials like these.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s8: See how he scorns human arguments, so that he will not take an oar, nor any other sail than his own wings, amid quarrels so distant.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s25: He answered me: "Just as I loved you in the mortal body, so I love you freed; therefore I stop; but you, why are you going?"
sentences.purgatorio-2-s27: "My Casella, to return once more to where I am I make this journey," I said; "but how is such an hour taken from you?"
sentences.purgatorio-2-s21: O vain shades — only in appearance!
sentences.purgatorio-2-s22: Three times I clasped her hands behind her, and just as many times I brought them back to my breast.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s23: Out of wonder, I think, I painted myself; whereupon the shade smiled and drew back, and I, following her, pressed onward.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s24: She sweetly said that I should rest; then I recognized who she was, and I begged that, in order to speak to me, she stay for a little.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s9: See how he holds them straight toward the sky, treating the air with his eternal feathers, which do not change like mortal hair.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s10: Then, as the divine bird came more and more toward us, it appeared the clearer; so that the eye could not bear it up close, but bowed downward; and it came to the shore of the one (thing) with a small, nimble and light boat, so that the water swallowed none of it.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s11: From the stern stood the heavenly helmsman, such that he would make one blessed merely described; and more than a hundred spirits sat within.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s12: 'In exitu Isräel de Aegypto' they sang all together with one voice, with all that of that psalm is afterwards written.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s17: Earlier we came a little ahead of you by another route which was so steep and severe that the ascent will now seem like a game.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s18: The souls, who became aware of me through my breathing — that I was still alive — marveling, became faint/pale.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s19: And as when a messenger bearing an olive draws the people to hear news, and no one shows himself reluctant to press forward, so all those blessed souls fixed themselves upon my face, almost forgetting to go and make themselves beautiful.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s20: I saw one of them draw herself forward to embrace me with such great affection that she moved me to do the same.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s13: Then he made the sign of the holy cross over them; whereupon they all threw themselves up onto the slope; and he went off, as he had come, swiftly.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s14: The crowd that remained there seemed savage because of the place, gazing around like someone who samples nine things.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s15: From every direction the sun shot its shafts through the day, which with its measured arrows had driven Capricorn from the middle of the sky, when the new people lifted up their faces toward us, saying to us, 'If you know, show us the way to go to the mountain.'
sentences.purgatorio-2-s16: And Virgil answered: 'Do you perhaps think that we are experienced in this place? But we are pilgrims just as you are.'
sentences.purgatorio-2-s1: By then the sun had already reached the horizon, whose meridian circle crowns Jerusalem at its highest point; and the night, opposite to it, came forth from the Ganges with the Scales (Libra), which let fall the hands when they are overborne; so that the white and the ruddy cheeks, where I was, by the fair Dawn through excessive age were becoming rancid.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s2: We were still far out on that sea, like people who think of their own journey, who go with the heart while the body remains behind.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s3: And behold: just as, surprised by the morning, Mars glows red through the heavy vapors down in the west above the sea-surface, so appeared to me — if I still see it — a light coming over the sea so swiftly that no flight equals its motion.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s4: After which, as I had drawn back my eye a little to call for my guide, I saw him again more shining and grown greater.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s37: What negligence — what kind of conduct is this?
sentences.purgatorio-2-s38: Run to the mountain to strip from yourselves the rock that prevents God from being manifest to you.
sentences.purgatorio-2-s39: Just as, when reaping grain or darnel, the doves gathered at the pasture — quiet, without showing their usual pride — if something appears that makes them afraid, they suddenly abandon the bait, because they are beset by greater anxiety; so I saw that fresh band leave the song and flee toward the shore, like a man who goes and does not know where he will come back; nor was our departure any less swift.
